coredata.dc:title: A Fourier graph neural network for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries simultaneously considering spatio-temporal features
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wanglin
Tian, Jindong
Li, Xiaoyu
Tian, Yong
Li, Guang
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries have been commonly applied in electric vehicles and renewable energy systems. To ensure the operating reliability and efficiency of lithium-ion batteries, it is imperative to estimate their state of health (SOH) online. Among various SOH estimation methods, data-driven methods have been given considerable attention. However, they often rely on time series neural networks, failing to capture inter-series (spatial) dynamics and intra-series (temporal) dependencies within health feature sequences. In this study, a Fourier graph neural network (FourierGNN) is proposed for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries. A hypervariable graph is constructed to represent the spatial and temporal correlations of multivariate features related to capacity degradation of lithium-ion batteries. The node dependencies in the hypervariable graph are refined into fully connected node-to-node dependencies, thus addressing the uncertainty and compatibility issues in spatiotemporal modeling, and establishing adaptive spatiotemporal dependencies. Experimental results show that the FourierGNN model performs well on multiple datasets. Compared with three existing neural networks, FourierGNN model averagely achieves about 35% and 52% reduction in MAE and RMSE errors on NASA B5 battery, respectively. In addition, when the models trained by NASA B5 battery dataset are directly applied to other batteries, FourierGNN can reduce the average MAE and RMSE errors about 50% and 46%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FourierGNN
Lithium-ion battery
State of health

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SOH degradation curves of different lithium-ion batteries.

Relationships between HIs and capacity of NASA B5 battery. (a), (b) and (c) Variation of ACVR, STCV and EVCT with the number of cycles. (d), (e) and (f) Correlations between ACVR, STCV and EVCT and battery capacity.

Hypergraph of lithium-ion battery characteristic factors. (a) Input windows of the three health feature sequences ACVR, STCV and ETCV. (b) Hypervariable graph constructed based on the features in (a) with the lines between nodes represent feature associations.
Flowchart of the Fourier graph convolutional neural network for SOH estimation.
The mean absolute error distribution of SOH estimation results with 30% training data.
SOH estimation results of FourierGNN model on: (a) B5, (b) B7, (c) CS35, (d) CS36, (e) NCA-1 and (f) NCA-2 batteries.
Comparison results of FourierGNN, LSTM, FEDformer and AGCRN on B7, CS2-35 and NCA-1 battery datasets.
Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients between HIs and capacity.
MAE and RMSE of different batteries with various ratios of training data.
Comparisons of different models on B5 dataset.
Generalization comparison results of different models.

head.ce:title: A Fourier graph neural network for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries simultaneously considering spatio-temporal features

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ), Shenzhen 518132, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ), Shenzhen 518132, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Manchester, M13 9PL, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Manchester, M13 9PL, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries have been commonly applied in electric vehicles and renewable energy systems. To ensure the operating reliability and efficiency of lithium-ion batteries, it is imperative to estimate their state of health (SOH) online. Among various SOH estimation methods, data-driven methods have been given considerable attention. However, they often rely on time series neural networks, failing to capture inter-series (spatial) dynamics and intra-series (temporal) dependencies within health feature sequences. In this study, a Fourier graph neural network (FourierGNN) is proposed for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries. A hypervariable graph is constructed to represent the spatial and temporal correlations of multivariate features related to capacity degradation of lithium-ion batteries. The node dependencies in the hypervariable graph are refined into fully connected node-to-node dependencies, thus addressing the uncertainty and compatibility issues in spatiotemporal modeling, and establishing adaptive spatiotemporal dependencies. Experimental results show that the FourierGNN model performs well on multiple datasets. Compared with three existing neural networks, FourierGNN model averagely achieves about 35% and 52% reduction in MAE and RMSE errors on NASA B5 battery, respectively. In addition, when the models trained by NASA B5 battery dataset are directly applied to other batteries, FourierGNN can reduce the average MAE and RMSE errors about 50% and 46%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para:
The dwindling of fossil energy sources and the escalating global warming crisis caused by excessive carbon dioxide emissions, underscores the urgency for an energy revolution [1]. Accordingly, the global focus has shifted to renewable energy, with the electric vehicle industry experiencing rapid growth worldwide [2]. Recent advancements in lithium-ion batteries [3], along with their advantages including minimal self-discharge, extended cycle life, high energy and power densities, wide operating temperature range, and eco-friendliness [4], have made them the preferred power source for electric vehicles. Typically, electric vehicles are equipped with a battery management system (BMS) [5], which is responsible for monitoring and controlling various state parameters of the batteries to ensure their safe operation within designated limits. SOH [6], reflecting the rate of capacity decay of lithium-ion batteries, is one of such important states to monitor and it is typically defined as:
(1)
SOH =
C k
C 0
× 100
where C 0 is the rated/nominal capacity and C
k denotes the actual capacity at the k-th cycle.

Accurate SOH estimation helps BMS predict battery operating performance, detect possible faults and take measures to protect the battery system from safety risks. Also, it guides BMS to adopt optimal charging/discharging strategies to maximize the use of remaining battery capacity and performance, as well as to extend battery useful life. With the increase in operating cycle, the battery capacity gradually decays [7] due to inner changes, such as the loss of active lithium ions (LLI), loss of active material (LAM) and increase in internal resistance, as well as external factors such as ambient temperature. For lithium-ion batteries used in electric vehicles, when their SOH is lower than 70%–80% [8], it is usually considered that the batteries have reached their end of life, and needs to be replaced.

To accurately predict battery SOH, various approaches have been developed, which are primarily categorized into model-based and data-driven methods [9]. Model-based approaches rely on physicochemical models or empirical formulations to describe the degradation process of batteries. These models typically include Mechanistic/Electrochemical Models, Equivalent Circuit Models (ECMs), and Mathematical Models [9,10]. Mechanistic models, which are grounded in electrochemical processes, use partial differential equations to simulate the dynamics of mass and charge transfer associated with battery aging. For example, Delacourt et al. [11] have extensively explored multi-scale physical modeling and numerical simulations of energy conversion and storage in electrochemical devices, providing valuable insights into battery aging through mathematical modeling. ECMs emulate battery charge–discharge behaviors using circuit elements. They capture the escalation of internal resistance over cycling due to the growth of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film, utilizing regression models combined with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to prognosticate battery health. Mathematical models, further categorized into battery degradation models and probability density function (PDF) models, describe battery capacity evolution over time and usage [9]. These models integrate various physical and chemical phenomena, such as electrode material dissolution and electrolyte diffusion, to predict capacity changes via mathematical formulations. PDF models establish probability distribution functions based on historical data analysis of battery capacity fluctuations. They provide probabilistic forecast of capacity changes, but lack detailed insights about battery internal physicochemical processes. Model-based methodologies offer a nuanced understanding of battery behaviors grounded in physical or chemical principles [12]. By considering complex physicochemical processes, they can realize accurate prediction, but require a lot of characteristic tests. Furthermore, model-based approaches are subject to simplified model assumptions or parameter uncertainties, and need constant update to accommodate various battery types and operating conditions. Conversely, data-driven approaches circumvent the need for extensive understanding of battery's internal mechanisms, and can derive SOH prediction models directly from battery test data [13].

Data-driven SOH estimation methods further include filter-based methods, statistical methods, and machine learning-based methods [14]. For filter-based methods, Mo et al. [15] proposed a fused approach for remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on particle filter combined with Kalman filter and particle swarm optimization (PSO), which was demonstrated to outperform standard particle filter and PSO-based particle filter. Park et al. [16] proposed an improved dual extended Kalman filter (DEKF) combined with parameter identification-based discrete derivative methods to address the issue of data-intensive requirements of Kalman filters. For statistical methods, Box et al. [17] utilized Gaussian process regression (GPR) for predicting battery health status. By integrating wavelet denoising strategy and Gaussian process functions, Richardson et al. [18] developed a hybrid Gaussian process function regression model to estimate battery SOH, which achieved higher flexibility, stability, and prediction accuracy compared to GPR. For machine learning-based methods, Nuhic et al. [19] proposed a support vector machine (SVM)-based model, in which battery health factors are extracted and fed into the SVM model for SOH prediction. Commonly used deep learning methods are often combined with time-series networks, such as recurrent neural network (RNN) and long short-term memory (LSTM). Eddahech et al. [20] utilized impedance spectroscopy and RNN to monitor lithium-ion battery behaviors and health status, enhancing prediction accuracy. Wang et al. [21] introduced an attention mechanism-based bidirectional LSTM framework to predict remaining useful life, achieving relative errors of 0.57%, 0.54%, 0.56%, 0.86%, 1.27%, and 1.41% on six types of lithium-ion batteries. Mazzi et al. [22] fused a 1D convolutional neural network with bidirectional gated recurrent units for SOH estimation, which achieved promising results. Chen et al. [23] proposed a SOH estimation method for lithium-ion batteries based on PSO and extreme learning machine (ELM). Obtained results indicated that the ELM model optimized by PSO achieved the average absolute percentage error within 1%. Li et al. [24] proposed a feature fusion-based hybrid neural network model to predict the SOH of lithium-ion batteries. The model parameters were optimized using Beluga Whale Optimization algorithm, and transfer learning was introduced to improve prediction accuracy and model generalization. However, deep learning methods with recurrent features have limitations when predicting SOH and RUL using long time-series data since they heavily rely on final vector outputs and do not allow the use of the entire time series during prediction. Existing data-driven methods struggle to effectively identify correlations between features. Space-time graph modeling has emerged as an essential task for analyzing spatial relationships and temporal trends in system components. Recent approaches tend to use space–time graph modeling to identify correlations between features. Kong et al. [25] utilized spatial graph neural networks (GNN) to combine spatial features with the structural characteristics of devices, introducing an enhanced attention mechanism to fuse temporal features for remaining useful life prediction. Suh et al. [26] proposed a two-stage scheme for remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion batteries by introducing a spatio-temporal multi-modal attention network (ST-MAN). This approach iteratively forecasts the number of cycles until battery end-of-life based on available data, with the ST-MAN model effectively capturing complex spatio-temporal dependencies. However, these approaches rely on two independent networks to capture temporal and spatial features, neglecting the holistic interpretation among battery features. To fill in above research gap, Cao et al. [27] introduced the Spectral Temporal Graph Neural Network (StemGNN), which jointly models inter-sequence and temporal dependencies in the spectral domain by integrating the graph Fourier transform for inter-sequence relationships and the discrete Fourier transform for temporal dependencies.

In summary, substantial research has been conducted on battery health assessment, offering valuable insights. While various methods demonstrate effectiveness, they also exhibit limitations in practical applications. For example, filter-based methods may suffer from sensitivity to noise and system dynamics uncertainties, lowering prediction accuracy in complex conditions. Statistical methods may fail to capture complex nonlinear relationships between data, particularly with limited or poor-quality data. Machine learning methods may encounter overfitting issues, especially with small training data or complex model structures, and require considerable computational resources. Deep learning methods, despite their effectiveness in capturing spatio-temporal relationships and nonlinear features, may depend heavily on large labeled datasets and lengthy training times, making them less interpretable [28,29]. State-of-the-art prediction methods based on GNNs typically rely on graph models (such as GCN) and temporal networks (like LSTM) to capture both inter-sequence (spatial) dynamics and intra-sequence (temporal) dependencies [30–32]. However, the uncertain compatibility between these networks creates additional challenges for model design. Furthermore, treating spatio-temporal dynamics separately overlooks the inherent interdependence of battery health features. Although StemGNN offers a promising solution, it is constrained by a fixed and predefined graph structure. This explicit graph may fail to represent the true dependency relationships, and missing connections in the data could result in the loss of important correlations.

This paper proposes a novel method using Fourier graph convolutional neural network (FourierGNN) for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries. By introducing the Fourier graph convolution operator (FGO), the proposed method can simultaneously extract information between various nodes in the hypervariable graph constructed by multiple battery feature sequences. Furthermore, it can effectively capture the relevance within and between multiple feature sequences. The main contributions of this study are summarized as follows:
1.
A FourierGNN neural model is proposed for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries based on three extracted health indicators. The proposed FourierGNN model can effectively capture intra- and inter-series correlations among exogenous variables (battery measurements). By treating each signal as a node within a fully connected graph structure, the FourierGNN can model spatial and temporal dependencies comprehensively. Furthermore, the framework can well balance learning ability and computational complexity.
2.
The proposed FourierGNN is validated comprehensively on three public datasets, involving various battery chemistries and test conditions. Results reveal that the FourierGNN framework can limit root mean square error below 3 ​% even only 30 ​% data are used for training. Additionally, the FourierGNN framework performs good generalization ability as it is applied directly to other batteries even with different test conditions and chemistries.
3.
The proposed FourierGNN is compared with three other neural networks, including both time-series and graph neural networks. Two experiments, involving different rations of training data and generalization on various datasets, are designed for comparison studies. Results demonstrate that compared with existing neural networks, FourierGNN model averagely achieves about 50% and 46% reduction in MAE and RMSE errors, respectively.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 first introduces the battery datasets used for verifying the proposed SOH estimation model, the selection of health indicators, and then details the design of the FourierGNN framework. Section 3 presents experimental results and discussions on the efficacy and superiority of the FourierGNN framework. Finally, Section 4 concludes the study with key findings and discusses future research directions.

To demonstrate the generalization of the SOH estimation model, this paper employs three publicly available lithium-ion battery datasets, including NASA PCoE Center [33], University of Maryland CALCE Center [34], and Tongji University [35]. The three battery datasets are briefly summarized as follows.
(1)
NASA battery dataset: It contains 34 lithium-ion battery cells, which are specified with a positive electrode of Lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) and a graphite-based negative electrode. All of the battery cells were charged with a constant current (CC) of 1.5 ​A at room temperature (24 ​°C) to an upper cut-off voltage of 4.2 ​V. Subsequently, charging continued and remained in constant voltage (CV) mode until the charging current dropped to 20 ​mA. For discharging experiments, the settings were slightly different. For instance, batteries 5, 6, 7, and 18 were discharged with a constant current of 2 ​A to a lower cut-off voltage of 2.7, 2.5, 2.2, and 2.5 ​V, respectively. Termination occurred when the batteries met the end of life standard, which is defined as a 30% reduction in rated capacity. Specifically, the available capacity of batteries 5, 6, 7, and 18 decreased from 2 to 1.4 Ah.
(2)
CALCE battery dataset: It consists of six subsets with various battery chemistries. In this paper, subset CS2 was used, which includes 15 LCO battery cells. Batteries underwent identical charging profiles, specifically following the standard CC-CV charging protocol. The constant current rate of 0.5C was remained until the battery voltage reached 4.2 ​V, at which the charging was transferred to the CV phase until the charging current dropped to 50 ​mA. For the discharging process, the batteries were discharged at various constant currents until the voltage fell to 2.7 ​V. For example, batteries CS2-35, CS2-36, CS2-37, and CS2-38 were discharged at a constant current rate of 1C.
(3)
Tongji University battery dataset. This study used Dateset_1 in the Tongji University data set. The tested object is nickel cobalt aluminum (NCA) oxide cell in 18650 formats, with the terminal voltage range of 2.65–4.2 ​V and the nominal capacity of 3.5Ah. The ambient temperature is 25 ​°C. The charging cycle consists of a CC phase with a current rate from 0.25C to 1C, and a CV phase of 4.2 ​V until the current dropped to 0.05C. Subsequently, a constant current is applied to discharge the batteries to 2.65 ​V. In the following sections, we will abbreviate batteries in the Tongji University battery dataset, Dataset_NCA_i as NCA-i, where i stands for the number of batteries.

To reduce the redundancy of experiments and design a more streamlined strategy, as few battery data as possible are selected while ensuring the accuracy, robustness and generalization of the proposed SOH estimation method. Particularly, batteries B5 and B7 from NASA PCoE, batteries CS2-35 and CS2-36 from the University of Maryland CALCE Center, and batteries NCA-1 and NCA-2 from Tongji University are selected. Fig. 1 depicts the battery SOH degradation curves. It is clear that the three battery types exhibit very different SOH degradation patterns.

The degradation characteristics of lithium-ion battery are often manifested as capacity decrease and internal resistance increase [36]. Capacity decay directly indicates the degradation of the battery health status. However, because of the complexity of online measurement or the absence of real-time measurement capabilities, especially in an incomplete charging/discharging scenario, capacity cannot be directly used for online prediction of lithium-ion battery SOH. Therefore, it is required to extract indirect health indicators (HIs) from measurable parameters, such as charging/discharging voltage, current, and temperature, etc. [37]. These parameters are related to battery capacity and internal resistance, and thus can reflect the battery health status. After analyzing the correlation between HIs and capacity decay, this study finally selected the following three HIs:
(1)
Average Charge Voltage Rise (ACVR)

ACVR represents the average change of charging voltage within in a predefined time interval. For a healthy battery, with the increase in charging time, battery voltage gradually increases, and the rate of voltage increase is relatively steady. As the battery ages, the internal resistance increases, resulting in a larger increase in battery voltage within the same charging time. As a result, in each charging cycle, the voltage at the same time point is higher than the previous cycle. Taking the 50th and 100th cycles of NASA B5 battery as an example, the voltage at 1,000 s are 3.945,6 and 4.021,8 ​V, respectively. In conclusion, ACVR combines the changes in charging voltage in different cycles and can reflect the overall battery performance degradation. Therefore, ACVR is introduced as a health indicator in this study. The ACVR at the i-th cycle is formulated as:
(2)
ACVR i
=
∑
j = 1
100
|
V j
−
V R
|
100
where V
j represents the j-th charging voltage within the time interval from 1,000 to 1,500 s, and V R represents the referenced charging voltage, which is 4.2 ​V for all the batteries in the three datasets used in this paper. The time interval from 1,000 to 1,500 s is selected because the ACVR within this period changes significantly with cycles. In addition, it covers the key moments from the CC charging stage to the CV charging stage, thereby capturing major charging characteristics of the lithium-ion batteries. The ACVR sequence for the total m charging cycles is:
(3)
ACVR =
ACVR 1
,
ACVR 2
,
⋅ ⋅ ⋅ ,
ACVR m
(2)
Saturation Time of Charging Voltage (STCV)

STCV is defined as the time required for the battery to be charged from an initial voltage (i.e., the first voltage at the beginning of charging) to an upper limit voltage (i.e., 4.2 ​V). As the charging cycle increases, the required charging time gradually decreases, reflecting a degradation in battery capacity. For a fresh battery, the charging voltage reaches the upper limit slowly. For an aging battery, the CC charging time to the upper cut-off voltage becomes shorter since capacity declines, i.e., reduced STCV. In summary, since the available capacity decreases gradually with the aging of batteries, battery charge amount within the constant current stage decreases, resulting in a shorter period of CC charging under the same charging conditions. Therefore, STCV is employed as another HI. The STCV sequence extracted from each charging cycle can be succinctly expressed as:
(4)
STCV =
STCV 1
,
STCV 2
,
⋅ ⋅ ⋅ ,
STCV m
where m is the charging–discharging cycles.
(3)
Equal Voltage Charging Time (EVCT)

EVCT stands for the charging time corresponding to an equal voltage range [7]. Specifically, this study sets the equal voltage range from 3.9 to 4.2 ​V. The physical explanation of EVCT is similar to STCV. The reason for choosing EVCT as an extra health indicator is that on the one hand, the data in the voltage range of 3.9–4.2 ​V is relatively flat, meaning less noises are included. On the other hand, such a partial voltage range is more practical because lithium-ion batteries, especially those used for electric vehicles, are subject to incomplete charging and discharging. Consequently, EVCT captures partial charging behaviors and reflects the performance changes of batteries under common usage patterns. The EVCT sequence with respect to m charging cycles can be described as:
(5)
EVCT =
EVCT 1
,
EVCT 2
,
⋅ ⋅ ⋅ ,
EVCT m

Among the three HIs, STCV mainly reflects the voltage changes throughout the entire CC phase of the charging process, which is directly related to the health status and degradation of the battery. ACVR and EVCT, on the other hand, focus on partial charging behaviors, which are more aligned with the actual application scenarios of lithium-ion batteries. The combination of these three HIs is to provide a more comprehensive and practical battery health status assessment. The three HIs capture the key characteristics of battery performance changes from two different perspectives: partial charging behavior and overall charging process. Taking NASA B5 battery as an example, the curves depicting the variations of ACVR, STCV, and EVCT with respect to cycles are presented in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that all the three HIs exhibit dramatical variations with the cycle, and they exhibit similar variation trends. Additionally, there are some correlations among the three HIs. For instance, the EVCT is a subset of the STCV. The aforementioned connections can be considered as spatial dependencies among HIs, and can be learned by the FourierGNN framework which will be introduced in Section 2.4.

In this study, Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients are employed to quantify the correlation between HIs and battery capacity, and they are formulated in Eqs. (6) and (7), respectively.
(6)
r xy
=
∑
X i
−
X ¯
Y i
−
Y ¯
∑
X i
−
X ¯
2
∑
Y i
−
Y ¯
2
(7)
r xy
=
E
X Y
− E
X
E
Y
E
X 2
−
E 2
X
E
Y 2
−
E 2
Y
where X and Y represent health indicators and overall capacity, respectively, while X
i and Y
i denote individual samples at i-th cycle.

Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients are used to measure the correlation between HIs and battery capacity. A larger absolute value indicates a stronger correlation, with an absolute value of 1 indicating perfect correlation. It is worth mentioning that when variables are continuous, normally distributed, and exhibit some degree of linear relationship, the Pearson correlation coefficient is preferred. If one of these conditions is satisfied, however, the Spearman correlation coefficient is more effective. Therefore, both coefficients are used here for a comprehensive evaluation and selection of health indicators.

ACVR, EVCT, and STCV are served as feature input sequences, HI1, HI2 and HI3, for predicting battery health status. The obtained Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients are summarized in Table 1 , which shows that all the correlation coefficients are above 0.95, demonstrating a high level of correlation between the health indicators and battery capacity. This high level of correlation suggests that the selected features are highly relevant to and effective in predicting battery health status.

Generally, the problem of battery SOH estimation using both graph network and temporal network can be formulated as:
(8)
Y ˆ
t
: =
F
θ g
,
θ t
X t
=
F
θ g
,
θ t
x
t − T + 1
, . . . ,
x t
where
Y ˆ
t
is the estimation value of SOH at timestamp t,
F
θ g
,
θ t
denotes mapping function of the whole network with parameters of graph network
θ g
for learning spatial correlations and parameters of temporal network
θ t
for learning temporal dependencies;
X t
∈
ℝ
N × T
represents a multivariate battery HI time-series window at timestamp t with the number of HIs being N (N ​= ​3 in this paper) and the window size of the time series being T.

Given a multivariate battery HI time series window as input
X t
∈
ℝ
N × T
, a hypervariable graph with N ​× ​T nodes can be constructed as
G t
=
X t G
,
A t G
. Here, each element in the sequence X
t is treated as a node in the graph G
t , thereby yielding
X t G
∈
ℝ
N T × 1
, and an NT ​× ​NT adjacency matrix
A t G
∈
ℝ
N T × N T
. Usually, the prior graph structure is unknown in most multivariate time series scenarios, and time-lagged effects cause elements in X
t are spatially or temporally correlated, so the hypervariable graph G
t is assumed to be fully-connected. The hypervariable graph G
t , consisting of NT nodes, is capable of learning high-resolution representations across timestamps and between feature variables.

With the constructed hypervariable graph G
t , estimation problem in Eq. (8) can be rewritten as:
(9)
Y ˆ
t
: =
F
θ G
X t G
,
A t G
where
θ G
represents the network parameters of the hypervariable graph. This representation allows us to view spatial correlations and temporal dependencies from a unified perspective, which is conducive to modeling the spatiotemporal interdependencies of multiple battery feature series.

The distinctive structure of the hypervariable graph captures both intra- and inter-correlations in the feature sequences, represented purely as node dependencies in the hypervariable graph. In contrast to conventional networks that separate spatial and temporal learning into two stages, the hypervariable graph structure integrates spatiotemporal correlations, thereby eliminating uncertainties inherent in spatial/temporal modeling and forming adaptive interdependencies across both dimensions.

Fig. 3 illustrates the hypervariable graph constructed by three sequences of health features, namely ACVR, STCV and EVCT. The window size of the time series, T, is an important parameter for the hypervariable graph. A larger window size can include more historical data and capture long-term dependencies better. Further, these long-term dependencies can reveal trends, seasonal changes, or cyclical behaviors in the data, thereby improving the SOH estimation accuracy. However, a larger window size requires higher computation cost, which will be analyzed explicitly in Section 2.4.2. In order to make a tradeoff between the estimation accuracy and computational complexity, a window size of 4 was determined in this paper after trial-and-error tests.

Although the hypervariable graph constructed in Section 2.4.1 can model spatial correlations and temporal dependencies from a unified perspective, it also poses some challenges. For example, if more health factors or/and larger window size are required, the order of the hypervariable graph will increase significantly. It leads to a very high computational complexity for the hypervariable graph and complicates the optimization of hidden nodes. This issue is very common for graph convolutional neural networks. FourierGNN provides an effective solution to it by introducing a Fourier graph operator (FGO) [28], which is a learnable network layer designed for the Fourier space. The definition of the FGO is briefed as follows.

Firstly, considering a graph G = (X, A) that has node features X ∈
ℝ
n × d
and an adjacency matrix A ∈
ℝ
n × n
, where n is the number of nodes and d is the number of features. Then a weight matrix W ∈
ℝ
d × d
is introduced to obtain a customized Green's kernel κ :
n
×
n
→
ℝ
d × d
with κ
i , j
: =
A
i j
∘ W and κ
i , j
= κ
i − j
. Further,
S
A , W
: = F
( κ )
∈
C
n × d × d
is defined as the FGO, where
F
represents the discrete Fourier transform (DFT). Since multiplication in the frequency domain is equivalent to convolution in the time domain, the multiplication of F
X
and F
κ
can be rewritten in the Fourier space as:
(10)
F
X
F
κ
= F
X ∗ κ
i
= F
∑
j = 1
n
X
j
κ
i − j
= F
∑
j = 1
n
X
j
κ
i , j
, ∀ i ∈
n
where
X ∗ κ
i
represents the convolution of X and κ . Based on the definition of κ
i , j
=
A
i j
∘ W , it can be deduced that
∑
j = 1
n
X
j
κ
i , j
=
∑
j = 1
n
A
i j
X
j
W = A X W . Accordingly, the convolution equation can be obtained as:
(11)
F
X
S
A , W
= F
A X W

Finally, the FourierGNN framework is obtained by stacking multiple layers of FGO as follows:
(12)
FourierGNN
X , A
: =
∑
k = 0
K
σ
F
X
S
0 : k
+
b k
,
S
0 : k
=
∏
i = 0
k
S i
where
S k
is the FGO in the k-th layer, satisfying F
X
S k
= F
A k
X
W k
.

Interpretability Analysis of FourierGNN: In the time domain, operation AXW employs different weights W to weigh the information of different neighbors in different diffusion orders, which is beneficial to capturing the extensive dependencies on graphs. As a result, the FourierGNN is capable of modeling the complex correlations among graph nodes, i.e., spatiotemporal dependencies among HIs. More details about the interpretability of FourierGNN and its applications in traffic, energy, web traffic, electrocardiogram and COVID-19 can be found in Ref. [28].

Complexity Analysis of FourierGNN: The computational complexity of FourierGNN has been demonstrated to be logarithmic-linear O
n
log
n
in Ref. [28]. It is much more efficient compared to the equivalent operations of
F
( X )
S
in the time domain, i.e., AXW, with the complexity of
O
(
d n
2
+ n
d 2
)
.
These results also reveal that although the FourierGNN can highly reduce computation burden by introducing FGO, its computation cost still is sensitive to the number of graph nodes (n), thereby being sensitive to both the number of health indicators (N) and window size of the time series (T) because n ​= ​N ​× ​T. Hence, it is necessary to set the number of health indicators and window size of the time series appropriately for balancing estimation accuracy and computation cost.

Fig. 4 summarizes the process of using FourierGNN for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries, including five steps:
(1)
The data is partitioned into time series windows of size T, obtaining health indicator sequence at timestamp t,
X t
∈
ℝ
N × T
.
(2)
The hypervariable graph corresponding to
X t
∈
ℝ
N × T
is constructed as G
t with the nodes
X t G
∈
ℝ
N T × 1
and an adjacency matrix
A t G
∈
ℝ
N T × N T
.
(3)
X t G
∈
ℝ
N T × 1
is mapped to a d-dimensional continuous space through a learnable embedding layer with a size of 1 ​× ​d, where d is set as 128 in this study to facilitate better feature learning, so that an (NT ​× ​d)-dimensional input matrix, i.e.,
X t G
∈
ℝ
N T × d
is obtained.
(4)
A DFT operation is implemented before inputting
X t G
to the FGO, after which the real and imaginary parts are computed separately through a learnable weight matrix in each stacked FGO, and then a multiplication sum is performed recursively for each FGO to obtain the estimation result
y t G
in the frequency domain.
(5)
Transforming
y t G
back to the time domain through Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT) yields
Y ˆ
t G
, which is then fed into a Fully Connected Network (FCN) to obtain the final SOH estimation results.

In summary, the FourierGNN model is designed in this paper to capture the spatio-temporal features of battery health indicators, which are strongly related to the underlying degradation mechanisms. Specifically, ACVR and STCV reflect the increase in internal resistance due to aging and the reduction in battery capacity with increasing cycle numbers. These phenomena are associated with the loss of active lithium ions (LLI) and the growth of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film. EVCT captures partial charging behavior within a specific voltage range. This is influenced by the same degradation factors as ACVR and STCV but is more relevant under actual usage patterns. The FGO in FourierGNN model allows the model to capture long-term dependencies and complex correlations among HIs in the frequency domain. Additionally, the fully connected node-to-node dependencies in the hypervariable graph enable the model to learn adaptive spatiotemporal relationships. These two aspects are key to linking model outputs with degradation mechanisms.

The training, validating and testing of neural networks were implemented on a personal computer, with a Windows 11 operation system, an AMD Ryzen 7 5800H CPU and 16 ​GB RAM. Python 3.8 and PyTorch programming environments were employed. Additionally, CUDA technology was utilized to accelerate and realize parallel computing on a single Quadro M1200 NVIDIA GPU.

Two indicators, namely mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) are used to assess the SOH estimation accuracy, and they are formulated as:
(13)
MAE =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y ˆ
i
−
y i
|
(14)
RMSE =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
y ˆ
i
−
y i
2
where N denotes the number of total samples,
y ˆ
i
and
y i
represent the estimated and actual SOH values with respect to the i-th training sample, respectively.

Firstly, SOH estimation accuracy of the proposed FourierGNN model is validated and discussed in this section. The specific experimental setup is as follows: the original data was firstly normalized using the min–max normalization strategy and then split into training, validation, and testing sets. The proportion of training data was set to three different levels, including 30%, 40%, and 50%, to assess the performance of the model with different amounts of input historical information. In terms of model parameter settings, after trial-and-error tunings, it was found that the setting of four FGO layers gave the best performance. For scenarios with different training set ratios, the ratios of corresponding validation and test sets are configured as follows: When the training set occupies 30%, the ratios of validation set and test set are 10% and 60%, respectively; When the training set occupies 40%, the ratios of validation set and test set are 10% and 50%, respectively; When the training set ratio is 50%, the ratios of validation set and test set are 10% and 40%. As an example, the SOH estimation results when the training set occupies 30% are presented in Fig. 5 . Also, SOH estimation errors in different cases are detailed in Table 2 .

Table 2 shows that both MAE and RMSE are lower than 3%, with the majority being within 2%. Particularly, the maximum MAE is 2.24% occurring on B5 battery with 30% training data and it is 2.65% for the RMSE occurring on CS2-36 battery also with 30% training data. The maximum errors for different batteries consistently occurred under the conditions of 30% training data, which reveals that increasing the ratio of training data is conducive to lowering SOH estimation error. It can be concluded that the FourierGNN-based model achieves precise SOH estimation and shows good generalization to different batteries.

Further, the proposed FourierGNN model was compared with three existing models, including LSTM [30], FEDformer [38] and Adaptive Graph Convolutional Recurrent Network (AGCRN) [39], on the B5 dataset. Among these existing models, LSTM is a typical and widely used time series network. FEDformer is a transformer model obtaining excellent results in the past two years. AGCRN is an excellent graph neural network that takes spatial relationships into consideration. Comparison results are detailed in Table 3 . It is indicated that the graph networks (AGCRN and FourierGNN) always achieve higher estimation accuracy than the other two temporal networks. Specifically, compared with AGCRN that performs best among the other three models, FourierGNN lowers the MAE from 1.12% to 0.67% when 50% data are used for training, achieving a decreasing amplitude of about 40.2%. Similarly, the RMSE using FourierGNN is reduced from 1.25% to 0.89%, with a decreasing amplitude of about 28.8%. These improvements in estimation accuracy demonstrate the effectiveness of the adaptive graph learning in capturing hidden spatial dependencies among feature sequences, thus achieving higher estimation accuracy. In summary, it is proved that the proposed FourierGNN network outperforms both the existing temporal networks and graph neural networks in estimation accuracy in the case of 50% training data. On average, FourierGNN reduces MAE and RSME errors by 35% and 52%, respectively. It is worth mentioning that although AGCRN is relatively unsensitive to the ratio of training data and even achieves higher accuracies under the conditions of 30% and 40% training data in comparison with FourierGNN, it requires much more computation cost as listed in Table 3, and its generalization ability is worse which will be presented in Section 3.2.2.

To demonstrate the generalization ability of the proposed FourierGNN model, the model trained only using 80% of B5 battery data was applied to estimate SOH of the other batteries directly. Here, using 80% of B5 battery data for model training is to obtain high-precise base models. Fig. 6 presents the SOH estimation results of three different types of batteries. The results in Fig. 6 indicate that the FourierGNN model consistently performed well across these datasets, despite differences in battery types, operating conditions, and environmental factors. It can be concluded that the FourierGNN model is able to capture the underlying patterns and associations among health features of lithium-ion batteries effectively, thereby achieving high robustness and reliability in real-world applications under diverse scenarios.

In order to further demonstrate the better generalization ability of the proposed FourierGNN model in comparison with other existing models, comparative experiments were conducted on B7, CS2-35 and NCA-1 datasets. The SOH estimation results are illustrated in Fig. 7 and corresponding errors are summarized in Table 4 . The results demonstrate that the FourierGNN network can model the battery SOH degradation better than the other three models, achieving the lowest MAE and RMSE within 1%. In particular, the average MAE and RMSE errors are reduced by about 50% and 46%, respectively. Furthermore, the errors of NASA battery datasets and Tongji University battery datasets are relative lower than that of CALCE battery datasets because they have similar SOH degradation curves.

In this study, a FourierGNN model that leverages the Fourier graph convolution has been proposed for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries. It overcomes the limitations of traditional time series and graph-based neural networks by constructing a hypervariable graph to capture both spatial and temporal dependencies. Experiments are carried out on three public battery datasets to verify the efficacy of the proposed FourierGNN model. Experimental results demonstrate that the FourierGNN model improves SOH estimation accuracy significantly, with an average reduction of about 35% and 52% in MAE and RMSE respectively, on NASA B5 battery dataset compared to existing models. The model robustness and generalization are further validated using multiple datasets. Results indicate that in comparison with existing neural networks, FourierGNN model averagely achieves about 50% and 46% reduction in MAE and RMSE errors, respectively. The adaptability and effectiveness of the FourierGNN model in capturing complex spatiotemporal dynamics makes it promising for enhancing battery health assessment and prognostics.

In the future, we will try to introduce more multivariate health features, involving voltage, current, temperature, acoustics, and mechanical stress, to further evaluate the efficacy and computation efficiency of the FourierGNN model. Also, parameters of the current model need to be further optimized in order to provide a more accurate and faster SOH estimator with less computation cost.

Wanglin Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jindong Tian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Data curation. Xiaoyu Li: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Yong Tian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Guang Li: Writing – review & editing.
Public data are used in this study, and the data and materials used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
